coredata.dc:title: Developing a novel optical sensor for condition assessment of aged mineral oil based on carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rashed, Abdellatif K.
Mansour, Diaa-Eldin A.
Rezk, A.
Ghali, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: Power transformers are major linking equipment in power systems, most of which use oil as insulating medium. This work aims to develop a sensitive opto-active material for effective condition assessment of mineral oil. Thermal aging is performed in the lab under conditions that simulate the actual operation of power transformers. Novel carbon dots are prepared in the lab to be used as an optical sensor for the aged mineral oil. The interaction between a fixed 2 mL of carbon dots and mineral oil samples aged for different times and at different concentrations is studied. A fixed oil concentration of 250 µL is selected and the emission spectra are linearly correlated with aging and dielectric dissipation factor. The in-service limits of dielectric dissipation factor are used to judge the mineral oil’s condition. The obtained photoluminescence spectra proved the suitability of this novel developed carbon dots for condition assessment of mineral oil.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Power transformer
Insulating mineral oil
Thermal aging
Dielectric dissipation factor
Photoluminescence spectroscopy
Carbon dots

head.ce:title: Developing a novel optical sensor for condition assessment of aged mineral oil based on carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Power transformers are major linking equipment in power systems, most of which use oil as insulating medium. This work aims to develop a sensitive opto-active material for effective condition assessment of mineral oil. Thermal aging is performed in the lab under conditions that simulate the actual operation of power transformers. Novel carbon dots are prepared in the lab to be used as an optical sensor for the aged mineral oil. The interaction between a fixed 2 mL of carbon dots and mineral oil samples aged for different times and at different concentrations is studied. A fixed oil concentration of 250 µL is selected and the emission spectra are linearly correlated with aging and dielectric dissipation factor. The in-service limits of dielectric dissipation factor are used to judge the mineral oil’s condition. The obtained photoluminescence spectra proved the suitability of this novel developed carbon dots for condition assessment of mineral oil.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: American Society for Testing and Materials
Wavelength at which the emission peak occurs (nm)
Referred wavelength at which the emission peak occurs
Real part of the dielectric constant.
Imaginary part of the dielectric constant

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Power transformers are of paramount importance in the electric grid. Whether stepping up or stepping down the voltage level, they are crucial for the appropriate power flow in the power system. Their good condition reflects a healthy power grid. A very good element to monitor and assess the condition of power transformers is their insulating medium which mostly is insulating oil. 70% of the diagnostic information resides in this insulating oil [1]. The insulating oil is crucial in providing the vital insulation medium in addition to being a cooling medium to dissipate heat from the transformer windings. Doubling the 20–35 year design life of power transformers is only possible through appropriate maintenance [2]. Considering the aforementioned points, condition assessment of insulating oil inside power transformers is paramount for a reliable operation.

Condition assessment aims to evaluate the degree of degradation that occurs in certain equipment during operation. This assessment is done using various electrical, chemical, thermal, and optical techniques. Good maintenance plans can be developed based on the data retrieved from these assessment techniques [3]. For instance, evaluating moisture content in the insulating oil is quite an expensive chemical technique for condition assessment that requires trained operators. Many methods were developed to attain the quantity of water content in the transformer oil [4]. Also, one of the sensitive electrical tests is the dielectric dissipation factor (DDF) test, which assesses the level of contamination in the insulating oil. This test is used and explained in detail in this work. Recently, optical spectroscopy has proved its effectiveness for condition assessment of insulating oil inside power transformers.

Optical spectroscopy is a non-traditional tool that could be utilized to assess the condition of the insulating medium in power transformers. Itis a non-destructive approach that does not require complex procedures or expertise to attain the results [5]. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy has been used extensively in the literature for various purposes. It has been used for condition monitoring [6], assessing the color index of the power transformer’s oil [7], measuring the interfacial tension of the power transformer’s oil [8], and many more. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is an immature method that could be used for condition monitoring of the insulating oil in power transformers. It has been adopted in [9], but it requires further investigation. These previous studies lack dealing with two major issues. The first issue is considering the oxidation process while performing accelerated aging of the insulating oil in the lab, which is intended to simulate the actual operating conditions of power transformers in service [9]. The second issue is the requirement for continuous and online monitoring in a modern smart grid. Currently, the samples are sent to the lab for optical spectroscopy analysis, which does not serve this purpose.

From this viewpoint, this study aims to develop a novel material to be used as an optical sensor for continuous online monitoring in power transformers. The effect of the increased oxidation level in power transformers in actual operation is taken into account with the help of a copper catalyst, as it will be shown later. This step is crucial to ascertain that the lab-prepared samples simulated the actual conditions of power transformers in service. A novel carbon dots (CDs) material sensitive to the aging process of mineral insulating oil is developed. The novel CDs are used to indicate aging and to be the basis for an online photoluminescence-based sensor for continuous condition assessment of the insulating oil inside power transformers.

CDs have gained more attention recently because of their easy and low-cost synthesis process. They are synthesized by putting an ultrasonically processed precursor through a hydrothermal process. The used precursor, time, and reaction temperature affect the size and properties of the prepared carbon dots, like their emission wavelength. Also, since they are biocompatible and non-poisonous, they have a wider range of applications. For example, novel synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots could be used to detect aniline -the carcinogenic harmful chemical- in water [10]. In this work, novel prepared CDs are used as an optical sensor to assess the insulating mineral oil condition. Due to their luminescence properties, these CDs have enabled the assessment and detection of emission spectra for aged mineral oil in extreme oxidation conditions. First, the emission spectra of the samples aged with and without oxidation are compared. Then, the CDs, the developed optical sensor, are used to obtain the emission spectra of the thermally aged mineral oil samples with oxidation under different aging durations. These emission spectra are compared for different concentrations of the aged mineral oil interacting with a fixed volume of the prepared CDs. Emission spectra parameters like the area under the PL spectra (APL) and the wavelength at which the emission peak occurs (λmax) are studied and correlated with variables like the DDF and aging time. Finally, the physical mechanisms behind the results are discussed and clarified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental process
ce:section.ce:para: First, the aging procedure performed in the lab for the insulating mineral oil will be explained. Then, the dielectric dissipation factor test will be introduced. After that, the photoluminescence spectroscopy test will be described. Lastly, the preparation of the novel CDs material will be explained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aging procedure
ce:section.ce:para:
Insulating mineral oil serves both the electrical insulation and thermal cooling functions inside power transformers. The followed criterion to thermally age this mineral oil in the lab is performed under conditions that simulate the real operating conditions of a power transformer in service. The thermal aging is performed in the lab at a temperature of 120 °C. One day at 120 °C is equivalent to 380 days for a power transformer in service [11]. This is better understood according to the half-life rule of thumb from the following equations [11]:
(1)
Aging
accelerating
factor =
120 ° C - 60 ° C
7 ° C
= 8.57
(2)
Time
factor =
2
8.57
= 380

Four samples were aged for 3, 6, 10, and 15 days with paper insulation and copper catalyst inside a sealed beaker. Also, one sample was aged for 6 days with only mineral oil in a sealed beaker. The sample aged for 6 days without copper catalyst or paper insulation is used for comparison. This comparison is done by obtaining the optical spectra and showing that the inclusion of paper insulation and copper catalyst makes the results vary greatly. In addition to that, the sample with the aging criterion that simulated the real operating conditions inside a power transformer will be selected for further optical tests.

Now, let’s explain the aging procedures for the four samples with paper insulation and copper catalyst. First, the typical power transformer’s oil-paper volume-weight ratio that was used is 20:1 [12]. In real power transformers, oxidation occurs, which is an inevitable process. In this study, the oxidation is simulated by adding a copper catalyst, which causes accelerated oxidation according to ASTM D 1934 [13]. Thus, through the inclusion of copper catalyst to accelerate oxidation and paper insulation in the aging procedure, the aged samples simulated the real operating conditions of power transformers in service. The procedures followed to age the four oil samples with paper insulation and copper catalyst are briefly explained as follows:
Step 1 The insulating paper was prepared with the typical ratio mentioned before, which is 20:1 oil-paper volume-weight ratio, and was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 48 h.
Step 2 The copper catalyst was prepared with the appropriate surface area according to the standard mentioned before, ASTM D 1934.

Step 3 The paper insulation was immersed carefully in the oil samples, and the whole beaker with the fresh mineral oil and the dried paper insulation was dried again in a vacuum oven at 45 °C for 24 h.
Step 4 The copper catalyst was added to the oil samples with paper insulation in them, and the beakers of the samples were sealed properly.
Step 5 The four samples were aged in a convection oven at 120 °C for 3, 6, 10, and 15 days, individually.
Step 6 Evaluation of the aged samples through dielectric and optical spectroscopy tests is performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dielectric dissipation factor test
ce:section.ce:para:
The dielectric dissipation factor (DDF) is the ratio between the imaginary part of the dielectric coefficient to the real part of the dielectric coefficient according to the equation:
(3)
tan
δ
=
ε
r
' '
ε
r
'

It represents the imperfection in the insulating mineral oil. The value of DDF would be zero for a perfect dielectric. In this study, the DDF was evaluated in accordance with the IEC 60247 standard [14]. The test was performed using a Keysight E4980A precision LCR meter. The test cell consists of two parallel electrodes with a diameter of 38 mm separated by 0.3 mm distance using an adjustable spacer. The oil sample is placed between the two electrodes. After that, the LCR meter calculates the equivalent parallel capacitance and equivalent parallel resistance of the oil sample over a frequency range from 20 Hz to 2 MHz. Then, the imaginary and real parts of the dielectric constant are calculated according to the equations:
(4)
ε
r
' '
=
t
2 × π × f ×
R
p
× A ×
ε
0
(5)
ε
r
'
=
t ×
C
p
A ×
ε
0

where t is the sample thickness, f is the frequency, A is the surface area of electrodes and
ε 0
is the permittivity of free space, while
C p
and
R p
are the parameters calculated by the LCR meter defined as the equivalent parallel capacitance and parallel resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence spectrometer
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectrometer consists of a light source that emits radiation, which then passes through optical lenses and is directed towards a single sample quartz cell using an optical element like a mirror. This cell is made of quartz so that it won’t absorb ultraviolet radiation. After that, the emission spectra are detected by a spectrophotometer. The spectrophotometer divides this emission into distinct wavelength components, and then the emission intensity is produced as a function of the wavelength. In this work, a Hitachi-2700 Fluorescence Spectrometer is used.

First, the sample cuvette with a 10 mm width is filled with the required sample. Then, the optical source of the PL spectrometer (Xenon lamp) was adjusted to emit radiation at the wavelength of 380 nm. The emission and excitation slits were adjusted at a resolution of 10 nm and 5 nm, respectively. Also, the photomultiplier operating voltage of the PL spectrometer was adjusted at 400 V. When the light beam makes a successful hit at the sample in the quartz cuvette, the structure is illuminated, and the electrons are excited to a higher energy state. When the electrons transit to the ground state, emission in the form of photons occurs. The monochromator disperses the released emission to a narrow band of wavelengths that are picked up by the detector. In the end, the software represents the PL spectrum with the y-axis as the emission intensity and the x-axis as the wavelength in nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs used as a PL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.3 g of dried fine pomegranate peel was transferred into a clean beaker, where 45 mL of distilled water was added. The solution was sonicated for 60 min and then transferred into a 750 mL Teflon-Line autoclave and heated at 100 ℃ for 10 h. The solution was allowed to cool naturally, and the large insoluble carbonaceous particles were filtered off using a 22 μm filter membrane. The CDs were collected by dialysis against deionized water through a dialyzing membrane for 48 h to remove carbohydrates, free amino acids and vitamins, ions, etc. The resultant supernatant was converted into powder using the rotary evaporator technique and stored at 4 0C [15]. These CDs are utilized in the solution form to study the solution interaction with the aged oil samples. This solution form of the CDs is obtained by dispersing the CDs in olive oil.

The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to study the crystallinity of the developed CDs. It is a known technique used to acquire information about the structural properties of materials. When a monochromatic X-ray beam strikes the sample, diffraction occurs, following the principles outlined by the Bragg equation:
(6)
2 d s i n ( θ ) = n λ

where λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray (1.54A), d is the inter-plane distance, θ is the scattering angle, and n is an integer called order of diffraction [16]. The inter-plane spacing is evaluated from the Bragg equation. The XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs is shown in Fig. 1 . As seen, a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 22.4° was observed with a d-spacing of 0.39 nm, which corresponds to the 002 planes of the graphitic structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of accelerated oxidation on PL spectra of aged oil samples
ce:section.ce:para: It was mentioned before in the description of aging procedure that two oil samples were aged for six days, but only one was aged without any paper insulation or copper catalyst. It was proven in previous work done with UV–Vis spectroscopy [17] that the aging criterion, which involved paper insulation and copper catalyst, successfully simulated the real operation of a power transformer in service. Also, it was proven that the effect of accelerated oxidation cannot be simply ignored. Moreover, the thermal aging of the mineral insulating oil, without considering the vigorous oxidation processes that occur naturally in a power transformer, is far from the real operating conditions. This work focused on developing the basis for a PL sensor. This is due to the fact that a PL online sensor is cost-effective and compact compared to a costly UV–Vis sensor that would be difficult to implement [18]. In the world of real applications, in order to utilize a PL sensor with the insulating mineral oil to assess its condition, the mineral oil has to be aged in conditions that are close to the real operating conditions of power transformers.

A look at the PL spectra for the 6-days aged oil samples shows the effect of including paper insulation and copper catalyst in the aging procedure. The PL spectra of the reference oil sample and the two samples aged for six days are shown in Fig. 2 . It is seen that the emission intensity drops with aging. It was observed that the maximum emission intensity dropped from 330 counts for the reference oil to 117 counts for the 6-days aged oil sample without copper catalyst or paper insulation. Also, the emission intensity dropped heavily from 330 counts for the reference oil sample to less than 5 counts for the 6-days aged oil sample with copper catalyst and paper insulation. This is a heavy drop observed in the emission intensity. It is expected now that the aged samples with copper catalyst and paper insulation have a very low emission intensity. The emission spectra for the four aged samples with copper catalyst and paper insulation in the aging procedure are shown in Fig. 3 . It is clear from the emission spectra that, as the aging time increased for the samples with copper catalyst and paper insulation in the aging procedure, the emission spectra dropped heavily, making the spectra less useful. This shows that, when attempting to replicate the real operating conditions of power transformers, the intensity of the emission spectra decreases heavily, making it inadequate to assess the condition of the insulating oil. While the spectra still contain valuable information, it is hard to characterize due to the heavily dropped emission intensity. This led to the idea of developing activating species to enhance the emission intensity in the PL spectra and utilize the valuable information in those spectra for condition assessment. This is the topic for the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced emission intensity of the aged oil samples by using novel synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: After showing the effect of including paper insulation and copper catalyst in the aging procedure and stating the problem of the dropped emission intensity of these samples, now the focus is on enhancing their emission intensity. The idea of the active medium revolves around enhancing the heavily dropped intensity and establishing a relationship with aging for condition assessment. Now, let’s investigate the interaction of the novel synthesized CDs, which were dispersed in olive oil, and the aged mineral oil. From the point of view of enhancing the intensity, the 6-days aged oil sample with copper catalyst and paper insulation was picked to see how it will interact with the novel developed CDs. Throughout the study, only 2 mL of CDs dispersed in olive oil were used, while the concentration of the aged mineral oil increased with a step of 50 μL. The different concentrations were meticulously measured by a microliter pipette. The pure solution of CDs is the CDs dispersed in olive oil without any additions of the mineral oil. In Fig. 4 , the enhanced emission intensity is shown for the 6-days aged oil sample with copper catalyst and paper insulation.

Two important conclusions could be drawn from Fig. 4. The first is that the emission intensity was enhanced by using the CDs material. The emission intensity took a huge leap from nearly 3 counts in the 6-days aged sample alone to almost 1000 counts in the pure CDs solution, which consists only of CDs dispersed in olive oil. The second important element is that the CDs material is responsive to the aged mineral oil, and different spectra are observed when changing the concentration of the aged mineral oil added to the CDs material. These two very important elements, the enhanced intensity and the sensitive selective response of the CDs to the changed concentration of the aged mineral oil, paved the way for further investigation into the matter. Moreover, when observing the effect of increasing the concentration of the aged oil added to the CDs material on the emission intensity, the 6-days aged sample was of interest. This is because the 6-days aged oil sample was the first to have a drop in emission intensity with the increment of the oil sample concentration. This promising CDs material will be further investigated for its suitability as an optical sensor in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Normalized emission spectra of various samples
Developed carbon dots interaction with reference oil
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of the CDs with the reference oil sample is represented in the normalized emission spectra shown in Fig. 5 . It is clear from the curves that the increment in oil concentration causes a shift in the maximum emission towards shorter wavelengths, which is a blue shift.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developed carbon dots interaction with 3-days aged oil
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of the CDs with the 3-days aged oil sample is represented in the normalized emission spectra presented in Fig. 6 (a). From the emission spectra of the 3-days aged oil sample, one could deduce that the increment in oil concentration caused a blue shift, but with a smaller step than the reference oil sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developed carbon dots interaction with 6-days aged oil
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of the CDs with the 6-days aged oil sample is represented in the normalized emission spectra given in Fig. 6 (b). The blue shift that occurred in the 3-days aged oil sample appears to be happening here also in the 6-days aged oil sample, but with even smaller step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developed carbon dots interaction with 10-days aged oil
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 6 (c), the interaction of the CDs with the 10-days aged oil sample is represented by the normalized emission spectra. It could be observed that the blue shift ceases to exist in this sample, and the spectra are concentrated around a single wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developed carbon dots interaction with 15-days aged oil
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of the CDs with the 15-days aged oil sample is represented by the normalized emission spectra shown in Fig. 6 (d). Now the trend of the wavelength shift is clear; first it was a blue shift for the reference sample, then this blue shift kept on decreasing till it ceased to exist with the 10-days aged oil sample. After that, with the 15-days aged oil sample, the increment of the aged oil concentration causes a red shift in the spectra. One could draw the conclusion that the 10-days aged oil sample is the point where the reversal of the spectral shift occurs because the spectra in this sample were fixed at one point.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral shift representation for the reference and aged oil samples with carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: It was shown how the spectral shift changes from blue shift in the reference oil sample to red shift in the 15-days aged oil sample. This trend is better understood if it is represented in a single plot. This behavior can be observed in Fig. 7 . The wavelength at which the emission peak of the pure CDs solution occurs is taken as a reference. Also, the spectral shift is calculated by subtracting the wavelength of this reference peak from the wavelength of the emission peak at oil concentration of 250 μL added to the 2 mL of the pure CDs solution. This is done for all aged oil samples and the reference oil sample. First, there is a blue shift, and then it becomes a red shift at the end with the 15-days aged oil sample added to the CDs. The crossover point occurred at an aging for 10 days. This linear correlation between the spectral shift and the aging time, that reaches a correlation coefficient of 0.98, is very intriguing and suggests the feasibility of an optical sensor. It is clear that the wavelength will be one of the parameters of interest in further investigation to put forward linear equations for an optical sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission intensity behavior for the reference and aged oil samples with carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding the effect of increasing the oil sample concentration added to the pure CDs solution on the emission intensity, it was mentioned above that the 6-days aged oil sample was the first experiencing a drop in emission intensity with the oil concentration increase. So, when studying the original emission spectra without normalization, the 6-days aged oil sample added to the pure CDs solution was the point where a reversal took place. Starting with the reference oil sample, the increment in the reference oil concentration added to the pure CDs solution caused the emission intensity to increase. Then, with the 3-days aged oil sample, the increment of its concentration added to the pure CDs solution caused the emission intensity to increase but with a smaller step. After that, with the 6-days aged oil sample, a reversal occurred, as any increase in the aged oil concentration caused the emission intensity to decrease. This drop was exacerbated with the 10-days aged sample, as the increased concentration of the aged oil added to the pure CDs solution caused a drop in emission intensity with a greater step. Moreover, with the 15-days aged oil sample, this drop in emission intensity was exacerbated even more. This behavior is clearly seen in Fig. 8 . First, the peak emission intensity of the pure CDs solution was taken as a reference. Then, the change in emission intensity for each oil sample was calculated by subtracting the peak emission intensity of the pure CDs solution from the peak emission intensity with oil concentration of 250 μL added to the 2 mL of the pure CDs solution. The change in emission intensity with aging time yielded a correlation coefficient of −0.9328, indicating a poor fit. A third-degree fit got a small error of 3.37%, but the error really vanishes with a fourth-degree fit to less than 0.01%. This proves that, unlike the spectral shift, the behavior of the change of emission intensity with aging time is nonlinear. The physical mechanism behind these observations will be discussed in the last section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed optical sensor at a fixed oil concentration of 250 μL
ce:section.ce:para: After understanding how the CDs spectra change with the aging time of oil samples, it is suitable to show the CDs behavior as an optical sensor to assess the condition of the insulating mineral oil. For this purpose, the fixed oil concentration of 250 μL is picked to compare the different oil samples at this selected concentration. First, the normalized emission spectra of the reference oil sample and the four aged oil samples are recorded, as shown in Fig. 9 . The behavior is clear; with aging, a red shift occurs in the emission spectra. In other words, the peak wavelength shifts to longer wavelengths with aging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission peak’s wavelength correlation with aging
ce:section.ce:para: The relation between the wavelength at which the emission peak occurs (λmax) and the aging time can be observed in Fig. 10 (a). There is a strong correlation coefficient of 0.98, which shows a nearly perfect linear dependence between the emission maximum wavelength and the aging time. This means that the introduced CDs could be used to indicate the aging of mineral insulating oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Area under the PL spectra correlation with aging
ce:section.ce:para: The relation between the area under the PL spectra (APL) and the aging time is presented in Fig. 10 (b). There is a strong correlation coefficient of 0.97, which shows a strong linear dependence between APL and the aging time. This shows that APL could also indicate oil aging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Area under the PL spectra correlation with DDF
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a correlation is established between the important electrical parameter which is DDF and the area under the PL spectra (APL). This is represented in Fig. 11 (a). The correlation coefficient is over 0.99, which is very impressive. This correlation proves the success of CDs as an optical sensor to assess the condition of the insulating mineral oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission peak’s wavelength correlation with DDF
ce:section.ce:para: The relation between the wavelength at which the emission peak occurs (λmax) and the DDF is given in Fig. 11 (b). There is a strong correlation coefficient of 0.99, which shows a nearly perfect linear relationship. This means that the introduced CDs can be used also to indicate important electrical parameters of the insulating mineral oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Judging mineral oil condition through DDF
ce:section.ce:para: It is now very important to judge the obtained results, and this will be done after choosing a reference. Fresh oil that has never been aged will be chosen as a suitable reference. Therefore, the wavelengths of different samples represented in Fig. 11 (b) will be divided by the wavelength of the reference oil sample at which the peak intensity occurs, which here is 441.84 nm. That way, the relation between the referred wavelength (λR) and DDF is presented in Fig. 12 (a). Similarly, the APL of various samples represented in Fig. 11 (a) will be divided by the APL of the reference oil sample, which here is 99.93 (a.u.). The relation between the referred area under the PL spectra (APLR) and DDF is represented in Fig. 12 (b).

The in-service limit for DDF is 0.005 for various voltage class power transformers, according to the IEEE Std C57.106–2015. However, with aging and due to thermal stresses, the value of DDF increases in a linear manner [6,19]. The DDF limit can be used to judge the aged oil samples. From Fig. 12 (a), it is clear that the sample aged for 15 days passes that limit, and therefore, it is not acceptable by the standard. Equations (7) and (8) are deduced from the fitted lines -with a correlation coefficient higher than 0.99- in Fig. 12 (a) and (b), respectively, as follows:
(7) y1 = α1 × x + β1
(8) y2 = α2 × x + β2

These equations are used to judge the status of the oil where α is the slope and β is the intercept value with the Y-axis. The constants α1 and β 1 have the values 13.9884 and 0.9824, respectively, while the constants α2 and β 2 have the values 55.2232 and 0.9428, respectively. It shall be noted that the four constants in equations (7) and (8) are unitless. For the in-service DDF limit value of 0.005, the limit values of λR and APLR
are 1.0523 and 1.2189, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical mechanisms and discussion
Basics of PL spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL) is the phenomenon of the emission of light. Emission occurs after the excitation of a molecule, typically by external radiation, resulting in a transition to an excited state. These electrons spend a certain time in the higher energy state, known as the lifetime, measured in nanoseconds. As the electrons relax back to the ground state, they emit their stored energy in the form of photons. The energy of the emitted photons is lower than that of the photons that exited the structure, according to Stokes's shift. This phenomenon could be explained by the famous Jablonski diagram [20], shown in Fig. 13 . The Quantum yield (QY) of the fluorescence process is defined as the number of emitted photons relative to the number of photons utilized in the absorption process. This QY is enhanced through doping with heteroatoms like nitrogen in a process called surface passivation [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction with the novel developed CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Efforts to explain the PL mechanism of CDs reached two main mechanisms: the quantum confinement effect and surface states-based explanations [21]. The latter explains the PL mechanism by surface chemistry. The functionalization through surface oxidative states by functional groups gives rise to specific fluorophores and energy levels, affecting the recombination and separation of electron-hole pairs. Also, the emission wavelength of the CDs is dependent on the extent of surface oxidation that gives rise to surface defects. It is established in the literature that carboxyl groups have an adverse effect that manifests in lowering the fluorescence intensity and PL quantum yield (QY) due to forming nonradiative recombination centers that act as electron acceptors. On the other hand, hydroxyl groups help in enhancing the PL QY by acting as electron donors and facilitating fast carrier mobility [22].

When explaining the emission spectra of the CDs interacting with the mineral oil, it could be deduced that the change in the chemical composition of the mineral oil through aging plays a crucial role in forming the surface chemistry of the CDs. The chemical structure of mineral oil, which is a complex mixture of hydrocarbon molecules, undergoes oxidation in the process of thermal aging. The oxidation process is considered a self-sustained reaction due to the autocatalytic mechanism. While oxidation increases with aging, the hydroxyl groups diminish, and the carboxyl groups dominate [23]. First, the hydroxyl groups in fresh mineral oil cause surface passivation to the CDs, resulting in a boost in the emission intensity and enhancing the PL QY. As the mineral oil ages, the carboxyl groups dominate gradually, creating nonradiative recombination centers that cause quenching in the emission spectra, thus lowering the PL QY. That is why at first, the increment in the reference oil concentration caused surface passivation and increased the PL QY of the CDs. While with samples aged for few days, there is still some degree of surface passivation, but it is quenched a bit.

When moving towards long aging times, the effect reverses, and the increment in oil concentration no longer causes surface passivation. Instead, it has an adverse effect on the QY of the CDs. This surface chemistry effect was reflected on the emission intensity in a nonlinear manner. In contrast, the aging process was reflected on the spectral shift in a linear manner, which forms the basis for an optical sensor. It started with a blue shift with the reference oil sample interacting with the CDs and finished with a red shift for the 15-days aged oil sample. The spectral shift is explained in the literature by something called the degree of oxidation, which is considered as one of the origins of fluorescence in CDs. An increase in surface defects caused by the increased degree of surface oxidation traps excitons and causes a red shift in emission. Also, there is a reduction in the bandgap with the increase of oxygen content on the CDs surface [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a novel approach was presented towards the condition assessment of the mineral insulating oil with the help of optical spectroscopy. The novel developed sensor was based on PL spectroscopy to facilitate any future implementation as an online sensor since a PL online sensor is cost-effective and compact compared to a costly UV–Vis sensor. Four mineral oil samples were aged in the lab with paper insulation and copper catalyst under conditions that simulated the real operating conditions of power transformers in service. One sample was thermally aged without copper catalyst or paper insulation to prove the importance of simulating the real operating conditions of power transformers. The PL spectra were helpful in the sample aged without copper catalyst or paper insulation. When taking into account the real operating conditions of power transformers, the intensity of the emission spectra drops heavily, making it unsuitable for condition assessment. The novel developed PL sensor is characterized by having activating species that enhance the heavily dropped emission intensity and enable a sensitive and selective response to changes in the aging time of mineral oil. The interaction of this novel developed material was investigated with the four aged oil samples with copper catalyst and paper insulation. A fixed criterion was followed in this study, as 2 mL of the pure CDs solution were used, and the concentration of the oil sample was increased with a step of 50 μL. When increasing the concentration of the reference oil sample with the CDs sensor, a blue shift was observed. This blue shift decreased with the 3-days aged oil sample and decreased even more with the 6-days aged oil sample. At 10 days of aging, no spectral shift was observed with increasing the oil concentration. Then, this blue shift reversed to a red shift with the 15-days aged oil sample. In order to investigate the applicability as an optical sensor for different durations of aged oil samples, a fixed oil concentration of 250 μL was chosen. The wavelength at which the emission peak occurs had a strong correlation coefficient of 0.98 with aging. Also, it had a correlation coefficient of 0.99 with the important electrical parameter DDF. Moreover, the area under the PL spectra had a correlation coefficient of 0.97 with aging and a correlation coefficient of 0.99 with DDF. The in-service limit of 0.005 for the DDF was used to judge the condition of the mineral oil. This shows that the emission spectra of the novel developed CDs optical sensor can be successfully used to indicate both aging and DDF for different aged mineral oil samples under aging conditions that simulate the real operating conditions of power transformers in service.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Abdellatif K. Rashed: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Conceptualization, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Diaa-Eldin A. Mansour: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. A. Rezk: Investigation, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Validation. Mohsen Ghali: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: D. A. Mansour appreciates the support from the Science, Technology and Innovation Funding Authority (STIFA), Egypt, through project ID 31292 and M. Ghali appreciates the support from STIFA through project ID 38311.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.measurement.2023.113956.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
